title: Pollo en Mole Chicken Mole Sauce
description: Pollo en Mole is a culinary masterpiece, a symphony of smoky chiles, aromatic spices, nuts, seeds, and a hint of chocolate, creating a sauce of unparalleled depth and richness. This iconic Mexican dish offers a complex flavor profile—simultaneously sweet, savory, earthy, and mildly spicy—enveloping tender chicken in a velvety, dark sauce that is deeply comforting and profoundly satisfying.

ingredients.notes: cut into 8 pieces, or 6-8 bone-in, skin-on chicken thighs and drumsticks
peeled and sliced, or 1/2 ripe banana
bolillo or similar, or 2 animal crackers for authentic flavor
e.g., Ibarra or Abuelita, roughly chopped

instructions.title: Cook the Chicken and Prepare Broth
instructions.text: Place chicken pieces in a large pot with quartered onion, smashed garlic, and salt. Cover with water or enough chicken broth to submerge. Bring to a boil, then reduce heat and simmer for 30-40 minutes, or until chicken is tender. Remove chicken and set aside. Strain the cooking liquid and reserve 6-8 cups of hot chicken broth.

instructions.title: Prepare and Rehydrate Chiles
instructions.text: Heat 1 tablespoon of lard or oil in a large skillet over medium-low heat. Briefly toast the stemmed, seeded, and deveined dried chiles for 15-30 seconds per side until fragrant but not burnt. Immediately transfer them to a bowl and cover with hot water. Let them rehydrate for 20-30 minutes until soft.

instructions.title: Toast Aromatics, Nuts, Seeds, and Spices
instructions.text: In the same skillet, add another tablespoon of lard or oil. Fry the chopped onion, garlic, tomatoes, tomatillos, and plantain until softened and lightly browned (about 8-10 minutes). Remove and set aside. Next, dry-toast the almonds, peanuts, sesame seeds, pumpkin seeds, raisins, peppercorns, cloves, cumin seeds, coriander seeds, cinnamon stick, and anise seeds (if using) separately, until fragrant. This should take 1-3 minutes per ingredient. Be careful not to burn the sesame seeds.

instructions.title: Blend the Mole Base
instructions.text: Drain the rehydrated chiles, reserving some soaking liquid. In a high-powered blender, combine the rehydrated chiles, fried aromatics/vegetables, toasted nuts, seeds, spices, torn corn tortilla, day-old bread, and chopped Mexican chocolate. Add 2 cups of the reserved hot chicken broth (or chile soaking liquid if needed). Blend until extremely smooth, adding more broth as necessary to achieve a thick, pourable paste. This may require blending in batches and passing through a fine-mesh sieve for ultimate smoothness (optional but recommended for traditional mole).

instructions.title: Fry the Mole Paste
instructions.text: Heat the remaining lard or oil in a large, heavy-bottomed pot or Dutch oven over medium heat. Carefully pour the blended mole paste into the hot fat. Be cautious as it will splatter. Fry the mole paste for at least 15-20 minutes, stirring constantly to prevent sticking. This crucial step deepens the flavor and color of the mole. The paste will darken and thicken considerably.

instructions.title: Simmer and Serve
instructions.text: Gradually add the remaining hot chicken broth to the fried mole paste, stirring constantly to incorporate. Bring the mole to a gentle simmer. Add the cooked chicken pieces to the mole sauce. Continue to simmer for at least 30-45 minutes, allowing the flavors to meld and the sauce to thicken to your desired consistency. Taste and adjust seasoning with salt, and a pinch of sugar if needed to balance the flavors. Serve hot, garnished with toasted sesame seeds, alongside Mexican rice and warm corn tortillas.

tips: Toasting Chiles Properly: Ensure chiles are toasted just until fragrant, not burnt, as burning will make the mole bitter. A quick pass over a comal or dry skillet is usually sufficient.

The Importance of Frying the Mole Paste: Frying the blended mole paste in lard (or oil) is non-negotiable for authentic flavor. This step deepens the color, intensifies the spice notes, and creates the characteristic richness of a true mole.

Achieving a Smooth Texture: For the silkiest mole, pass the blended sauce through a fine-mesh sieve or a chinoise after blending. While time-consuming, it ensures a luxurious mouthfeel free of any fibrous chile bits.
article:
## History of Pollo en Mole Chicken Mole Sauce

Mole, particularly Mole Poblano, is perhaps Mexico's most iconic and complex culinary creation, with a history as rich and layered as its flavor. Its origins are often traced back to the convents of Puebla in the 17th century, where nuns are said to have created the elaborate sauce to honor visiting dignitaries. Legend attributes its invention to Sor Andrea de la Asunción or Sor Pascuala, who, faced with an unexpected visit from an archbishop, combined various ingredients in a moment of inspired desperation. However, the roots of mole run much deeper, predating the Spanish conquest, with indigenous populations creating sophisticated sauces from chiles, seeds, and spices. The addition of Old World ingredients like nuts, bread, and chocolate after the arrival of the Spanish transformed these ancient pastes into the intricate moles we know today, making Pollo en Mole a true testament to Mexico's mestizo culinary heritage.

## Why This Recipe Works

This recipe works by meticulously building layers of flavor, a hallmark of authentic Mexican mole. The initial toasting of dried chiles enhances their smoky and fruity notes, while rehydration makes them pliable for blending. Separately toasting nuts, seeds, and spices brings out their essential oils and aromatic compounds, contributing depth and complexity. The crucial step of frying the blended mole paste in lard (or oil) triggers the Maillard reaction, transforming simple ingredients into a rich, dark, and intensely flavored sauce. Finally, the slow simmering with chicken broth allows all the diverse components—from the earthy chiles to the subtle sweetness of chocolate and raisins—to meld into a harmonious, velvety sauce that perfectly coats the tender chicken, creating a truly unforgettable dish.

## Chef Tips for Perfection

For a truly perfect Pollo en Mole, pay close attention to the individual preparation of each ingredient. When toasting chiles, work quickly and avoid burning them, as this will introduce a bitter taste. Toast nuts, seeds, and spices separately and to their optimal fragrance; this unlocks their full flavor potential. A high-powered blender is essential for achieving a super-smooth mole paste, but don't hesitate to pass it through a fine-mesh sieve if you desire an ultra-silky texture. The frying of the mole paste is arguably the most critical step; stir constantly for at least 15-20 minutes until the paste darkens and thickens, indicating the flavors are fully developed. Lastly, use good quality Mexican table chocolate, as its unique flavor profile is integral to the authentic taste of mole.

## Serving Suggestions

Pollo en Mole is a dish meant to be savored, and its traditional accompaniments enhance the experience. Serve the rich chicken mole alongside fluffy white or red Mexican rice, which provides a perfect neutral base to soak up every drop of the complex sauce. Warm, freshly made corn tortillas are indispensable for scooping up the chicken and sauce. For a refreshing contrast, offer a sprinkle of toasted sesame seeds over the top and a side of simple, thinly sliced pickled red onions or a light salad. A sprinkle of crumbled queso fresco can also add a touch of salty creaminess. For beverages, a traditional agua fresca or a Mexican cerveza complements the meal beautifully.

jsonLd.name: Pollo en Mole Chicken Mole Sauce
jsonLd.description: Pollo en Mole is a culinary masterpiece, a symphony of smoky chiles, aromatic spices, nuts, seeds, and a hint of chocolate, creating a sauce of unparalleled depth and richness. This iconic Mexican dish offers a complex flavor profile—simultaneously sweet, savory, earthy, and mildly spicy—enveloping tender chicken in a velvety, dark sauce that is deeply comforting and profoundly satisfying.
jsonLd.keywords: Mexican, Mexican, authentic, traditional, chicken, Mexican, authentic, traditional, chicken, mole
recipeIngredient: 1 (3-4 lbs) whole chicken (cut into 8 pieces, or 6-8 bone-in, skin-on chicken thighs and drumsticks)
1 Chipotle chile (stemmed, seeded (optional, for extra heat/smokiness))
1/2 cup white onion (roughly chopped, for mole)
1 small ripe plantain (peeled and sliced, or 1/2 ripe banana)
2 tablespoons sesame seeds (plus more for garnish)
2 slices day-old bread (bolillo or similar, or 2 animal crackers for authentic flavor)
50g (approx. 1/4 tablet) Mexican table chocolate (e.g., Ibarra or Abuelita, roughly chopped)
a pinch sugar (optional, to balance flavors)
